message.content: I'd like your help thinking through and defining a 'style guide' for a particular character of sequence: an orchestration sequence.  I've done several of these and I'm starting to have opinions about solid specification language approach.  For our initial focus, please find the windows garrison BUS0 subdocument, and also lets look at RBS0 subdocuments concerning orchestration of google cloud builder context.  Would you locate and read such documents?  A good clue is whether you have found documents with `_call` related quoins.  Lets start by finding the right precedents.

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: Good — the `_call` clue points to RB Bash Console Control (`rbbc_call` = CALL), defined at RBS0:430 and used pervasively in the ark family. Let me pull the candidate orchestration specs in parallel.

content.content:
1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_garrison_wsl}
3	
4	NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with WSL bash as the workload shell, for
5	Windows nodes. Workload identity inhabits *three namespaces*: a Windows
6	local user (auth boundary discovered by Windows OpenSSH), the workload's
7	*own* registered WSL distribution `rbtww-main` (populated in workload's
8	`HKCU\Lxss` via `wsl --import` from a seed tarball), and a Linux user
9	inside that workload-owned distribution (execution context entered by
10	the locked-down `command=` directive's `wsl.exe --user`). The third
11	namespace is forced by Microsoft's per-user WSL design -- distributions
12	register only in the running user's `HKCU\Lxss`; admin's distributions
13	are invisible to workload sessions.
14	
15	NOTE: Mechanism is *SSH-as-workload conducted by the privileged
16	orchestrator* against an *admin-owned absolute-path authorized_keys*.
17	{busn_caparison} installs `Match User ${BUJB_workload_user}` in
18	`sshd_config` routing `AuthorizedKeysFile` to
19	`__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/${BUJB_workload_user}/authorized_keys` --
20	the documented Microsoft pattern (parallel to the built-in
21	`Match Group administrators` redirect to
22	`administrators_authorized_keys`). Sshd's trust-file lookup never
23	resolves the workload's `$HOME`, so workload SSH auth is *decoupled*
24	from any workload-profile state -- a future profile demotion cannot
25	lock out auth. Garrison writes the bare workload pubkey to that admin
26	path in admin context, opens a *single* workload SSH session to import
27	`rbtww-main` into workload's `HKCU\Lxss`, mint the inner Linux user,
28	plant the privkey, then runs `wsl.exe --shutdown` as the final act
29	before session exit to release the `NTUSER.DAT` hive cleanly, then
30	rewrites the absolute-path authorized_keys with the locked-down
31	`command=` form (still admin context). The single-session-with-shutdown
32	discipline keeps the Windows User Profile Service demotion path closed
33	during garrison-w itself, so `wsl --import` lands in the canonical
34	profile's `HKCU\Lxss` and not a numbered fallback dir
35	(`bujuw_user.<host>.NNN`). Round-trip validation under the locked-down
36	directive is deferred to {bust_invigilate_windows}. The brief
37	bare-authorized_keys window exposes only the operator's own running
38	garrison -- workload pubkey derived from operator-owned
39	{burp_workload_key_file}.
40	
41	NOTE: Destruction is letter-agnostic via `zbujb_obliterate_workload` in
42	{bujb_jurisdiction} -- single greppable destroy verb running the
43	five-phase nuclear cleanup: hive release (`wsl --shutdown` +
44	helper-process teardown) → `Win32_UserProfile` WMI delete (atomic
45	`ProfileList` SID, `NTUSER.DAT`, and folder removal including
46	numbered fallback dirs) → SAM scrub (`Remove-LocalUser`) → filesystem
47	sweep (any leftover `C:\Users\<user>*` dirs and the
48	`C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\` Cygwin home) → WSL vestige cleanup inside
49	admin's `rbtww-main` (vestige from prior garrison shapes; the
50	workload's own distribution lives in the workload's `HKCU\Lxss`).
51	Idempotent on absent state and tolerant of partial prior destruction.
52	Best-effort: Windows User Profile Service performs asynchronous
53	internal bookkeeping (`.bak` SID lifecycle, mounted-state markers)
54	after profile removal, so a 100% pristine state is not always
55	achievable on a single pass; the discipline is to sweep thoroughly
56	enough that `New-LocalUser` lands on a clean SID with the canonical
57	profile path. Admin trust is {busn_caparison}'s scope; sshd hardening
58	inside the WSL distribution remains operator-manual. Documentation
59	sources for the cleanup pattern: Microsoft Learn ("Profile loading
60	fails", "Scripts to clean profile folder and prevent TEMP user profile
61	creation"); helgeklein.com (Delprof2 -- documented authority for safe
62	profile teardown sequencing).
63	
64	The {busn_garrison} operation on Windows via WSL:
65	
66	//axhopt_typed_parameter
67	{burp_investiture}
68	
69	//axhoq_precondition
70	A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
71	{burn_regime} targets a Windows OpenSSH node, reachable as
72	{burp_privileged_user} under key-only auth using
73	{burp_privileged_key_file}.
74	
75	//axhoq_precondition
76	{busn_caparison} has run for this {burp_investiture} -- admin SSH trust
77	established, `rbtww-main` staged under the admin user (canonical:
78	`BUJB_wsl_distribution` in {bujb_jurisdiction}), operator-managed
79	registry posture / Tailscale / powercfg all in the shape
80	{bust_invigilate_windows} expects, AND `sshd_config` carries the
81	`Match User ${BUJB_workload_user}` block routing `AuthorizedKeysFile`
82	to `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/${BUJB_workload_user}/authorized_keys`
83	(with the parent directory pre-provisioned, admins-FullControl +
84	workload-Read ACL).
85	
86	//axhoq_precondition
87	{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
88	station.
89	
90	//axhos_step
91	. *Assert admin posture*:
92	.. {busc_call}
93	{bust_invigilate_windows} for this {burp_investiture}
94	.. {busc_store}
95	`«INVIGILATE_RESULT»`
96	.. {busc_require}
97	`«INVIGILATE_RESULT»` is zero
98	.. {busc_fatal}
99	on non-zero -- {bust_handbook_windows} scope (operator-managed) or
100	{bust_caparison_windows} scope (caparison-managed); diagnostic surfaced
101	verbatim
102	
103	//axhos_step
104	. *Open admin SSH session*:
105	.. {busc_call}
106	ssh as {burp_privileged_user} with {burp_privileged_key_file}; key-auth only
107	.. {busc_store}
108	`«ADMIN_SESSION»`
109	.. {busc_fatal}
110	on connection failure -- {bust_caparison_windows} scope
111	
112	//axhos_step
113	. *Export admin's rbtww-main as workload-readable seed*:
114	.. {busc_call}
115	PowerShell `wsl.exe --export rbtww-main <seed-path>` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»`
116	to a path inside the workload's Windows profile -- admin can write there
117	before the workload first logs in, workload session can read it
118	.. {busc_store}
119	`«SEED_TARBALL»` (the chosen path)
120	.. {busc_fatal}
121	on export failure -- {bust_caparison_windows} scope (rbtww-main missing
122	or admin HKCU\\Lxss corrupted)
123	
124	//axhos_step
125	. *Obliterate prior workload*:
126	.. {busc_call}
127	`zbujb_obliterate_workload` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»` -- five-phase
128	nuclear cleanup. Each phase is composed of discrete atomic admin SSH
129	calls per WSG WSp-105 (one cmdlet or one native binary per SSH body;
130	bash drives probe + decide + per-target dispatch between calls).
131	SSH sessions are cheap; compound PowerShell bodies are not used. All
132	phases idempotent on absent state and tolerant of partial prior
133	destruction. The workload's authorized_keys at
134	`__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/${BUJB_workload_user}/authorized_keys`
135	lives outside the workload profile and is *not* touched by this step;
136	caparison provisioned the directory, step 6 overwrites the file
137	contents
138	.. *Phase 1 -- Release hives* (two SSH calls): first call
139	`wsl.exe --shutdown` (host-wide WSL VM teardown); second call
140	`Stop-Process -Name wslhost,wslservice -Force -ErrorAction
141	SilentlyContinue` for helpers that survive shutdown. Releases
142	`NTUSER.DAT` handles held by WSL helpers from any previous
143	garrison-w run. Without this phase, Windows User Profile Service can
144	demote a subsequent workload logon to a numbered fallback profile
145	directory (`bujuw_user.<host>.NNN`), rewriting `ProfileImagePath` in
146	`HKLM\...\ProfileList\<SID>` -- a permanent corruption that
147	survives reboot
148	.. *Phase 2 -- Profile destruction via `Win32_UserProfile`* (probe +
149	iterate + per-target dispatch): probe call
150	`Get-CimInstance -ClassName Win32_UserProfile -Filter "LocalPath LIKE
151	'C:\\Users\\${BUJB_workload_user}%' OR LocalPath LIKE
152	'C:\\Users\\${BUJB_workload_user}.%'"` -- two `LIKE` patterns catch
153	canonical AND numbered fallback dirs from prior demotions; emits one
154	row per matching profile. Bash parses `LocalPath` per row, then for
155	each row issues one Remove call:
156	`Remove-CimInstance -Query "SELECT * FROM Win32_UserProfile WHERE
157	LocalPath = '<row.LocalPath>'"`. This is the documented Microsoft
158	path: User Profile Service handles `ProfileList` SID key removal
159	(canonical AND `.bak` variants), `NTUSER.DAT` hive unload, and folder
160	deletion atomically
161	.. *Phase 3 -- SAM scrub* (probe + conditional dispatch): probe call
162	`Get-LocalUser -Name '${BUJB_workload_user}' -ErrorAction
163	SilentlyContinue` -- emits the user object if present, empty if
164	absent. Bash checks emptiness; if present, dispatch call
165	`Remove-LocalUser -Name '${BUJB_workload_user}'`. Fresh local user is
166	created in step 5 claiming a new SID; removing the old SAM entry
167	severs lingering references and guarantees the next `New-LocalUser`
168	is not a no-op
169	.. *Phase 4 -- Filesystem sweep* (belt-and-braces for paths that
170	survived Phase 2): Windows-side probe call
171	`Get-ChildItem -Path 'C:\\Users' -Filter '${BUJB_workload_user}*'
172	-Directory -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` emits zero or more
173	directory rows; bash iterates, and for each row dispatches one call
174	`Remove-Item -Recurse -Force -LiteralPath '<row.FullName>'`. Cygwin
175	probe call `Test-Path 'C:\\cygwin64\\home\\${BUJB_workload_user}'`;
176	bash branches on `True` / `False`, and on `True` dispatches
177	`Remove-Item -Recurse -Force
178	'C:\\cygwin64\\home\\${BUJB_workload_user}'`
179	.. *Phase 5 -- WSL vestige*: workload's Linux user inside admin's
180	`rbtww-main` distribution -- vestige from prior garrison shapes;
181	the workload's own distribution lives in its own `HKCU\Lxss`,
182	populated in step 7. Probe call
183	`wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root id
184	${BUJB_workload_user}` -- exit 0 if user present, non-zero if absent.
185	Bash checks exit; if present, dispatch call
186	`wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root userdel
187	${BUJB_workload_user}`. Always-run cleanup call
188	`wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root rm -rf
189	/home/${BUJB_workload_user}` (orphan home from prior partial garrison).
190	Each `wsl.exe` invocation is one native binary call (WSp-105 #2);
191	inner shell delegation via `wsl.exe ... bash -c "..."` (WSp-105 #3)
192	is acceptable but unnecessary at this scope
193	.. NOTE: Cleanup is best-effort. Windows User Profile Service
194	performs asynchronous bookkeeping (`.bak` SID lifecycle,
195	mounted-state markers) so a 100% pristine state is not always
196	achievable on a single pass. The discipline is to sweep thoroughly
197	enough that step 5's `New-LocalUser` lands on a clean SID with the
198	canonical profile path; incremental residue does not affect
199	operational behavior
200	.. References for the nuclear cleanup pattern: Microsoft Learn
201	("Profile loading fails", "Scripts to clean profile folder and
202	prevent TEMP user profile creation"); helgeklein.com (Delprof2)
203	.. {busc_fatal}
204	on any individual SSH call's failure -- {bust_handbook_windows}
205	scope
206	
207	//axhos_step
208	. *Create Windows workload account*:
209	.. provision {bujb_workload_user} as a Windows local user via
210	`net.exe user /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»`
211	.. register the resulting SID under
212	`HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList` so
213	Windows OpenSSH builds a complete logon session on inbound SSH --
214	Win32-OpenSSH #1383
215	.. no Linux user is created inside admin's `rbtww-main` -- the
216	workload's Linux user is minted inside the workload's *own* imported
217	distribution in step 7
218	.. {busc_fatal}
219	on Windows account-management failure -- prior obliterate left state
220	
221	//axhos_step
222	. *Place bare workload trust*:
223	.. {busc_call}
224	`ssh-keygen -y` on {burp_workload_key_file}
225	.. {busc_store}
226	`«WORKLOAD_PUBKEY»` from stdout
227	.. write `«WORKLOAD_PUBKEY»` to the admin-owned absolute path
228	`__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/${BUJB_workload_user}/authorized_keys`
229	over `«ADMIN_SESSION»` as a bare entry (no `command=` directive) --
230	locked-down `command=` would route through workload's `HKCU\Lxss`
231	before the import lands. Directory pre-provisioned by
232	{busn_caparison} with admins-FullControl + workload-Read ACL;
233	single `Set-Content` cmdlet per WSp-105
234	.. file is admin-owned throughout its lifetime, no ownership-transfer
235	gymnastics; ACL parallel to `administrators_authorized_keys`
236	satisfies sshd's Windows-OpenSSH StrictModes-equivalent check.
237	Caparison's `Match User ${BUJB_workload_user}` block in `sshd_config`
238	routes `AuthorizedKeysFile` to this path directly -- no `$HOME`
239	resolution involved at any point in workload auth
240	.. {busc_fatal}
241	on key-write failure -- workload account left without trust
242	
243	//axhos_step
244	. *Provision workload's WSL (single SSH-as-workload session)*:
245	.. {busc_call}
246	ssh as {bujb_workload_user} with {burp_workload_key_file} through the
247	bare entry at the absolute-path authorized_keys -- session is a real
248	Windows logon for the workload, HKCU mounted. This is the *only*
249	SSH-as-workload session inside garrison-w; the lockdown rewrite
250	moves to step 8 (admin context, same absolute path), and round-trip
251	validation under the locked-down directive is deferred to
252	{bust_invigilate_windows}
253	.. {busc_store}
254	`«WORKLOAD_SESSION»`
255	.. over `«WORKLOAD_SESSION»` run `wsl.exe --import rbtww-main
256	<workload-wsl-root> «SEED_TARBALL»` -- registers the workload's own
257	`rbtww-main` in workload's `HKCU\Lxss`; `<workload-wsl-root>` is a
258	workload-writable directory inside the workload's Windows profile
259	.. then `wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root useradd
260	--create-home --shell /bin/bash ${BUJB_workload_user}` -- mints the
261	inner Linux workload user
262	.. then `wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root passwd --lock
263	${BUJB_workload_user}` -- enforces SSH-key-only on the inner Linux
264	account
265	.. then plant {burp_workload_key_file}'s contents at
266	`/home/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/id_ed25519` (chmod 600) inside the
267	distribution -- workload's outbound auth, e.g., GitHub
268	.. *Final act before session exit*: `wsl.exe --shutdown` --
269	terminates the workload's WSL VM and helper processes (`wslhost.exe`,
270	`wslservice`), releasing the `NTUSER.DAT` hive handle BEFORE Windows
271	unloads the logon session. Required even with absolute-path
272	authorized_keys: `wsl --import` registered state in the canonical
273	profile's `HKCU\Lxss`, and a stray demotion during this session
274	would relocate the registration to a fallback profile that
275	subsequent operational sessions would not see (auth would still
276	succeed by virtue of the absolute-path authorized_keys, but
277	`wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main` in the locked-down `command=`
278	would fail to find the distribution)
279	.. on session exit, delete `«SEED_TARBALL»` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»` --
280	seed has served its purpose
281	.. {busc_fatal}
282	on workload session failure, `wsl --import` failure, inner `useradd` /
283	`passwd` failure, privkey plant failure, `wsl --shutdown` failure,
284	or seed delete failure -- workload state partially provisioned across
285	namespaces; revalidate from a clean garrison
286	
287	//axhos_step
288	. *Lock down workload trust (admin rewrite)*:
289	.. over `«ADMIN_SESSION»` overwrite the absolute-path authorized_keys
290	at
291	`__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/${BUJB_workload_user}/authorized_keys`,
292	replacing the bare entry from step 6 with the locked-down `command=`
293	form -- single `Set-Content` cmdlet per WSp-105. File is admin-owned
294	throughout; no ownership-transfer gymnastics, no in-session rewrite
295	race
296	.. canonical directive content: `BUJB_command_w` in {bujb_jurisdiction}
297	with `${BUJB_workload_user}` substituted at directive-emit time:
298	+
299	[listing]
300	----
301	command="C:/Windows/System32/wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user ${BUJB_workload_user} --exec /bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
302	----
303	.. {busc_fatal}
304	on rewrite failure -- bare trust still in place; subsequent workload
305	SSH would bypass the locked-down route. Revalidate from a clean
306	garrison
307	
308	//axhog_guarantee
309	On success:
310	
311	* Exactly one workload identity named {bujb_workload_user} exists,
312	  mirrored across all three namespaces.
313	* Windows local user with SID registered under `HKLM\...\ProfileList`
314	  (auth boundary).
315	* Workload-owned `rbtww-main` registered in workload's `HKCU\Lxss`
316	  (distribution namespace).
317	* WSL Linux user inside that distribution (execution context entered
318	  by the locked-down `command=` directive's `wsl.exe --user`).
319	* Workload authorized_keys at
320	  `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/${BUJB_workload_user}/authorized_keys`
321	  carries `«WORKLOAD_PUBKEY»` with the shell-letter `w` `command=`
322	  directive -- admin-owned absolute path; auth lookup decoupled
323	  from workload `$HOME` via caparison-installed `Match User
324	  ${BUJB_workload_user}` block.
325	* {burp_workload_key_file} planted at `/home/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/id_ed25519`
326	  inside the workload's distribution.
327	* `wsl.exe --shutdown` executed inside the workload session before
328	  exit; no orphan `NTUSER.DAT` hive handles outlive garrison-w.
329	* Admin posture is unchanged -- no `sshd_config` writes, no admin
330	  authorized_keys writes (caparison-managed Match block governs
331	  the workload authorized_keys location).
332	
333	Garrison-w does *not* self-validate the locked-down round-trip;
334	{bust_invigilate_windows} asserts the round-trip as a post-completion
335	fact. Operator runs invigilate after garrison-w to confirm the
336	locked-down directive routes end-to-end through workload's WSL.
337	
338	//axhoc_completion
339	Exit 0 when the admin-context lockdown rewrite at the absolute-path
340	authorized_keys (step 8) completes, after `wsl.exe --shutdown` from
341	the workload session and seed cleanup. Non-zero on invigilate-windows
342	precondition failure, admin SSH failure, seed export failure,
343	obliterate failure (any phase or per-target SSH call), Windows
344	account-management failure, bare key-write failure, workload SSH
345	session failure, `wsl --import` failure, inner account-management
346	failure, privkey plant failure, hive-release failure, seed cleanup
347	failure, or lockdown rewrite failure.

//axhob_operation
{bust_garrison_wsl}

NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with WSL bash as the workload shell, for
Windows nodes. Workload identity inhabits *three namespaces*: a Windows
local user (auth boundary discovered by Windows OpenSSH), the workload's
*own* registered WSL distribution `rbtww-main` (populated in workload's
`HKCU\Lxss` via `wsl --import` from a seed tarball), and a Linux user
inside that workload-owned distribution (execution context entered by
the locked-down `command=` directive's `wsl.exe --user`). The third
namespace is forced by Microsoft's per-user WSL design -- distributions
register only in the running user's `HKCU\Lxss`; admin's distributions
are invisible to workload sessions.

NOTE: Mechanism is *SSH-as-workload conducted by the privileged
orchestrator* against an *admin-owned absolute-path authorized_keys*.
{busn_caparison} installs `Match User ${BUJB_workload_user}` in
`sshd_config` routing `AuthorizedKeysFile` to
`__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/${BUJB_workload_user}/authorized_keys` --
the documented Microsoft pattern (parallel to the built-in
`Match Group administrators` redirect to
`administrators_authorized_keys`). Sshd's trust-file lookup never
resolves the workload's `$HOME`, so workload SSH auth is *decoupled*
from any workload-profile state -- a future profile demotion cannot
lock out auth. Garrison writes the bare workload pubkey to that admin
path in admin context, opens a *single* workload SSH session to import
`rbtww-main` into workload's `HKCU\Lxss`, mint the inner Linux user,
plant the privkey, then runs `wsl.exe --shutdown` as the final act
before session exit to release the `NTUSER.DAT` hive cleanly, then
rewrites the absolute-path authorized_keys with the locked-down
`command=` form (still admin context). The single-session-with-shutdown
discipline keeps the Windows User Profile Service demotion path closed
during garrison-w itself, so `wsl --import` lands in the canonical
profile's `HKCU\Lxss` and not a numbered fallback dir
(`bujuw_user.<host>.NNN`). Round-trip validation under the locked-down
directive is deferred to {bust_invigilate_windows}. The brief
bare-authorized_keys window exposes only the operator's own running
garrison -- workload pubkey derived from operator-owned
{burp_workload_key_file}.

NOTE: Destruction is letter-agnostic via `zbujb_obliterate_workload` in
{bujb_jurisdiction} -- single greppable destroy verb running the
five-phase nuclear cleanup: hive release (`wsl --shutdown` +
helper-process teardown) → `Win32_UserProfile` WMI delete (atomic
`ProfileList` SID, `NTUSER.DAT`, and folder removal including
numbered fallback dirs) → SAM scrub (`Remove-LocalUser`) → filesystem
sweep (any leftover `C:\Users\<user>*` dirs and the
`C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\` Cygwin home) → WSL vestige cleanup inside
admin's `rbtww-main` (vestige from prior garrison shapes; the
workload's own distribution lives in the workload's `HKCU\Lxss`).
Idempotent on absent state and tolerant of partial prior destruction.
Best-effort: Windows User Profile Service performs asynchronous
internal bookkeeping (`.bak` SID lifecycle, mounted-state markers)
after profile removal, so a 100% pristine state is not always
achievable on a single pass; the discipline is to sweep thoroughly
enough that `New-LocalUser` lands on a clean SID with the canonical
profile path. Admin trust is {busn_caparison}'s scope; sshd hardening
inside the WSL distribution remains operator-manual. Documentation
sources for the cleanup pattern: Microsoft Learn ("Profile loading
fails", "Scripts to clean profile folder and prevent TEMP user profile
creation"); helgeklein.com (Delprof2 -- documented authority for safe
profile teardown sequencing).

The {busn_garrison} operation on Windows via WSL:

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition
A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
{burn_regime} targets a Windows OpenSSH node, reachable as
{burp_privileged_user} under key-only auth using
{burp_privileged_key_file}.

//axhoq_precondition
{busn_caparison} has run for this {burp_investiture} -- admin SSH trust
established, `rbtww-main` staged under the admin user (canonical:
`BUJB_wsl_distribution` in {bujb_jurisdiction}), operator-managed
registry posture / Tailscale / powercfg all in the shape
{bust_invigilate_windows} expects, AND `sshd_config` carries the
`Match User ${BUJB_workload_user}` block routing `AuthorizedKeysFile`
to `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/${BUJB_workload_user}/authorized_keys`
(with the parent directory pre-provisioned, admins-FullControl +
workload-Read ACL).

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
station.

//axhos_step
. *Assert admin posture*:
.. {busc_call}
{bust_invigilate_windows} for this {burp_investiture}
.. {busc_store}
`«INVIGILATE_RESULT»`
.. {busc_require}
`«INVIGILATE_RESULT»` is zero
.. {busc_fatal}
on non-zero -- {bust_handbook_windows} scope (operator-managed) or
{bust_caparison_windows} scope (caparison-managed); diagnostic surfaced
verbatim

//axhos_step
. *Open admin SSH session*:
.. {busc_call}
ssh as {burp_privileged_user} with {burp_privileged_key_file}; key-auth only
.. {busc_store}
`«ADMIN_SESSION»`
.. {busc_fatal}
on connection failure -- {bust_caparison_windows} scope

//axhos_step
. *Export admin's rbtww-main as workload-readable seed*:
.. {busc_call}
PowerShell `wsl.exe --export rbtww-main <seed-path>` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»`
to a path inside the workload's Windows profile -- admin can write there
before the workload first logs in, workload session can read it
.. {busc_store}
`«SEED_TARBALL»` (the chosen path)
.. {busc_fatal}
on export failure -- {bust_caparison_windows} scope (rbtww-main missing
or admin HKCU\\Lxss corrupted)

//axhos_step
. *Obliterate prior workload*:
.. {busc_call}
`zbujb_obliterate_workload` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»` -- five-phase
nuclear cleanup. Each phase is composed of discrete atomic admin SSH
calls per WSG WSp-105 (one cmdlet or one native binary per SSH body;
bash drives probe + decide + per-target dispatch between calls).
SSH sessions are cheap; compound PowerShell bodies are not used. All
phases idempotent on absent state and tolerant of partial prior
destruction. The workload's authorized_keys at
`__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/${BUJB_workload_user}/authorized_keys`
lives outside the workload profile and is *not* touched by this step;
caparison provisioned the directory, step 6 overwrites the file
contents
.. *Phase 1 -- Release hives* (two SSH calls): first call
`wsl.exe --shutdown` (host-wide WSL VM teardown); second call
`Stop-Process -Name wslhost,wslservice -Force -ErrorAction
SilentlyContinue` for helpers that survive shutdown. Releases
`NTUSER.DAT` handles held by WSL helpers from any previous
garrison-w run. Without this phase, Windows User Profile Service can
demote a subsequent workload logon to a numbered fallback profile
directory (`bujuw_user.<host>.NNN`), rewriting `ProfileImagePath` in
`HKLM\...\ProfileList\<SID>` -- a permanent corruption that
survives reboot
.. *Phase 2 -- Profile destruction via `Win32_UserProfile`* (probe +
iterate + per-target dispatch): probe call
`Get-CimInstance -ClassName Win32_UserProfile -Filter "LocalPath LIKE
'C:\\Users\\${BUJB_workload_user}%' OR LocalPath LIKE
'C:\\Users\\${BUJB_workload_user}.%'"` -- two `LIKE` patterns catch
canonical AND numbered fallback dirs from prior demotions; emits one
row per matching profile. Bash parses `LocalPath` per row, then for
each row issues one Remove call:
`Remove-CimInstance -Query "SELECT * FROM Win32_UserProfile WHERE
LocalPath = '<row.LocalPath>'"`. This is the documented Microsoft
path: User Profile Service handles `ProfileList` SID key removal
(canonical AND `.bak` variants), `NTUSER.DAT` hive unload, and folder
deletion atomically
.. *Phase 3 -- SAM scrub* (probe + conditional dispatch): probe call
`Get-LocalUser -Name '${BUJB_workload_user}' -ErrorAction
SilentlyContinue` -- emits the user object if present, empty if
absent. Bash checks emptiness; if present, dispatch call
`Remove-LocalUser -Name '${BUJB_workload_user}'`. Fresh local user is
created in step 5 claiming a new SID; removing the old SAM entry
severs lingering references and guarantees the next `New-LocalUser`
is not a no-op
.. *Phase 4 -- Filesystem sweep* (belt-and-braces for paths that
survived Phase 2): Windows-side probe call
`Get-ChildItem -Path 'C:\\Users' -Filter '${BUJB_workload_user}*'
-Directory -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` emits zero or more
directory rows; bash iterates, and for each row dispatches one call
`Remove-Item -Recurse -Force -LiteralPath '<row.FullName>'`. Cygwin
probe call `Test-Path 'C:\\cygwin64\\home\\${BUJB_workload_user}'`;
bash branches on `True` / `False`, and on `True` dispatches
`Remove-Item -Recurse -Force
'C:\\cygwin64\\home\\${BUJB_workload_user}'`
.. *Phase 5 -- WSL vestige*: workload's Linux user inside admin's
`rbtww-main` distribution -- vestige from prior garrison shapes;
the workload's own distribution lives in its own `HKCU\Lxss`,
populated in step 7. Probe call
`wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root id
${BUJB_workload_user}` -- exit 0 if user present, non-zero if absent.
Bash checks exit; if present, dispatch call
`wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root userdel
${BUJB_workload_user}`. Always-run cleanup call
`wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root rm -rf
/home/${BUJB_workload_user}` (orphan home from prior partial garrison).
Each `wsl.exe` invocation is one native binary call (WSp-105 #2);
inner shell delegation via `wsl.exe ... bash -c "..."` (WSp-105 #3)
is acceptable but unnecessary at this scope
.. NOTE: Cleanup is best-effort. Windows User Profile Service
performs asynchronous bookkeeping (`.bak` SID lifecycle,
mounted-state markers) so a 100% pristine state is not always
achievable on a single pass. The discipline is to sweep thoroughly
enough that step 5's `New-LocalUser` lands on a clean SID with the
canonical profile path; incremental residue does not affect
operational behavior
.. References for the nuclear cleanup pattern: Microsoft Learn
("Profile loading fails", "Scripts to clean profile folder and
prevent TEMP user profile creation"); helgeklein.com (Delprof2)
.. {busc_fatal}
on any individual SSH call's failure -- {bust_handbook_windows}
scope

//axhos_step
. *Create Windows workload account*:
.. provision {bujb_workload_user} as a Windows local user via
`net.exe user /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»`
.. register the resulting SID under
`HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList` so
Windows OpenSSH builds a complete logon session on inbound SSH --
Win32-OpenSSH #1383
.. no Linux user is created inside admin's `rbtww-main` -- the
workload's Linux user is minted inside the workload's *own* imported
distribution in step 7
.. {busc_fatal}
on Windows account-management failure -- prior obliterate left state

//axhos_step
. *Place bare workload trust*:
.. {busc_call}
`ssh-keygen -y` on {burp_workload_key_file}
.. {busc_store}
`«WORKLOAD_PUBKEY»` from stdout
.. write `«WORKLOAD_PUBKEY»` to the admin-owned absolute path
`__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/${BUJB_workload_user}/authorized_keys`
over `«ADMIN_SESSION»` as a bare entry (no `command=` directive) --
locked-down `command=` would route through workload's `HKCU\Lxss`
before the import lands. Directory pre-provisioned by
{busn_caparison} with admins-FullControl + workload-Read ACL;
single `Set-Content` cmdlet per WSp-105
.. file is admin-owned throughout its lifetime, no ownership-transfer
gymnastics; ACL parallel to `administrators_authorized_keys`
satisfies sshd's Windows-OpenSSH StrictModes-equivalent check.
Caparison's `Match User ${BUJB_workload_user}` block in `sshd_config`
routes `AuthorizedKeysFile` to this path directly -- no `$HOME`
resolution involved at any point in workload auth
.. {busc_fatal}
on key-write failure -- workload account left without trust

//axhos_step
. *Provision workload's WSL (single SSH-as-workload session)*:
.. {busc_call}
ssh as {bujb_workload_user} with {burp_workload_key_file} through the
bare entry at the absolute-path authorized_keys -- session is a real
Windows logon for the workload, HKCU mounted. This is the *only*
SSH-as-workload session inside garrison-w; the lockdown rewrite
moves to step 8 (admin context, same absolute path), and round-trip
validation under the locked-down directive is deferred to
{bust_invigilate_windows}
.. {busc_store}
`«WORKLOAD_SESSION»`
.. over `«WORKLOAD_SESSION»` run `wsl.exe --import rbtww-main
<workload-wsl-root> «SEED_TARBALL»` -- registers the workload's own
`rbtww-main` in workload's `HKCU\Lxss`; `<workload-wsl-root>` is a
workload-writable directory inside the workload's Windows profile
.. then `wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root useradd
--create-home --shell /bin/bash ${BUJB_workload_user}` -- mints the
inner Linux workload user
.. then `wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root passwd --lock
${BUJB_workload_user}` -- enforces SSH-key-only on the inner Linux
account
.. then plant {burp_workload_key_file}'s contents at
`/home/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/id_ed25519` (chmod 600) inside the
distribution -- workload's outbound auth, e.g., GitHub
.. *Final act before session exit*: `wsl.exe --shutdown` --
terminates the workload's WSL VM and helper processes (`wslhost.exe`,
`wslservice`), releasing the `NTUSER.DAT` hive handle BEFORE Windows
unloads the logon session. Required even with absolute-path
authorized_keys: `wsl --import` registered state in the canonical
profile's `HKCU\Lxss`, and a stray demotion during this session
would relocate the registration to a fallback profile that
subsequent operational sessions would not see (auth would still
succeed by virtue of the absolute-path authorized_keys, but
`wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main` in the locked-down `command=`
would fail to find the distribution)
.. on session exit, delete `«SEED_TARBALL»` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»` --
seed has served its purpose
.. {busc_fatal}
on workload session failure, `wsl --import` failure, inner `useradd` /
`passwd` failure, privkey plant failure, `wsl --shutdown` failure,
or seed delete failure -- workload state partially provisioned across
namespaces; revalidate from a clean garrison

//axhos_step
. *Lock down workload trust (admin rewrite)*:
.. over `«ADMIN_SESSION»` overwrite the absolute-path authorized_keys
at
`__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/${BUJB_workload_user}/authorized_keys`,
replacing the bare entry from step 6 with the locked-down `command=`
form -- single `Set-Content` cmdlet per WSp-105. File is admin-owned
throughout; no ownership-transfer gymnastics, no in-session rewrite
race
.. canonical directive content: `BUJB_command_w` in {bujb_jurisdiction}
with `${BUJB_workload_user}` substituted at directive-emit time:
+
[listing]
----
command="C:/Windows/System32/wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user ${BUJB_workload_user} --exec /bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
----
.. {busc_fatal}
on rewrite failure -- bare trust still in place; subsequent workload
SSH would bypass the locked-down route. Revalidate from a clean
garrison

//axhog_guarantee
On success:

* Exactly one workload identity named {bujb_workload_user} exists,
  mirrored across all three namespaces.
* Windows local user with SID registered under `HKLM\...\ProfileList`
  (auth boundary).
* Workload-owned `rbtww-main` registered in workload's `HKCU\Lxss`
  (distribution namespace).
* WSL Linux user inside that distribution (execution context entered
  by the locked-down `command=` directive's `wsl.exe --user`).
* Workload authorized_keys at
  `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/${BUJB_workload_user}/authorized_keys`
  carries `«WORKLOAD_PUBKEY»` with the shell-letter `w` `command=`
  directive -- admin-owned absolute path; auth lookup decoupled
  from workload `$HOME` via caparison-installed `Match User
  ${BUJB_workload_user}` block.
* {burp_workload_key_file} planted at `/home/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/id_ed25519`
  inside the workload's distribution.
* `wsl.exe --shutdown` executed inside the workload session before
  exit; no orphan `NTUSER.DAT` hive handles outlive garrison-w.
* Admin posture is unchanged -- no `sshd_config` writes, no admin
  authorized_keys writes (caparison-managed Match block governs
  the workload authorized_keys location).

Garrison-w does *not* self-validate the locked-down round-trip;
{bust_invigilate_windows} asserts the round-trip as a post-completion
fact. Operator runs invigilate after garrison-w to confirm the
locked-down directive routes end-to-end through workload's WSL.

//axhoc_completion
Exit 0 when the admin-context lockdown rewrite at the absolute-path
authorized_keys (step 8) completes, after `wsl.exe --shutdown` from
the workload session and seed cleanup. Non-zero on invigilate-windows
precondition failure, admin SSH failure, seed export failure,
obliterate failure (any phase or per-target SSH call), Windows
account-management failure, bare key-write failure, workload SSH
session failure, `wsl --import` failure, inner account-management
failure, privkey plant failure, hive-release failure, seed cleanup
failure, or lockdown rewrite failure.

1	//axhob_operation
2	{rbtgo_trigger_build}
3	
4	NOTE: This operation dispatches a container build via the vessel's
5	{rbtgr_vessel_trigger}
6	using the committed
7	{rbtgr_build_json}
8	in its
9	{rbtgr_vessel_dir}.
10	All substitution values are baked at inscribe time — zero custom overrides at dispatch.
11	
12	NOTE: This operation supports concurrent execution per the Concurrent Session Safety invariant.
13	Each invocation dispatches an independent trigger execution with a unique `BUILD_ID`.
14	Concurrent builds of the same vessel produce independent images with different
15	{rbtga_hallmark}
16	timestamps.
17	
18	The
19	{rbtr_director}
20	dispatches a trigger-based remote container build:
21	
22	//axhopt_typed_parameter
23	{rbtgr_vessel_dir}
24	. *Validate Input Parameters*:
25	.. {rbbc_require}
26	`«INPUT_VESSEL_DIR»` provided as first argument
27	.. {rbbc_require}
28	`«INPUT_VESSEL_DIR»` is an existing directory
29	.. {rbbc_store}
30	`«VESSEL_ENV_FILE»` as `«INPUT_VESSEL_DIR»/rbrv.env`
31	.. {rbbc_require}
32	`«VESSEL_ENV_FILE»` exists
33	.. {rbbc_fatal}
34	if missing: "Vessel configuration not found"
35	
36	//axhos_step
37	. *Load Vessel Configuration*:
38	.. Source `«VESSEL_ENV_FILE»` into current environment
39	.. {rbbc_require}
40	{rbrv_sigil} defined and non-empty
41	.. {rbbc_store}
42	`«VESSEL_SIGIL»` from {rbrv_sigil}
43	.. Validate vessel directory name matches `«VESSEL_SIGIL»`
44	.. {rbbc_fatal}
45	if mismatch: "Vessel sigil does not match directory name"
46	
47	//axhos_step
48	. *Validate Conjuring Configuration*:
49	.. {rbbc_require}
50	{rbrv_conjure_dockerfile} defined
51	.. {rbbc_require}
52	{rbrv_conjure_bldcontext} defined
53	.. {rbbc_fatal}
54	if missing: "Vessel not configured for conjuring"
55	
56	//axhos_step
57	. *Authenticate as
58	{rbtr_director}*:
59	.. Execute
60	{rbtoe_director_authenticate}
61	.. {rbbc_store}
62	`«DIRECTOR_TOKEN»` for subsequent API calls
63	.. {rbbc_store}
64	`«DEPOT_PROJECT_ID»` as `${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}d-${RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER}` (resolved by `RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID`)
65	.. {rbbc_store}
66	`«GAR_REPOSITORY»` as `${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}${RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER}-gar` (resolved by `RBDC_GAR_REPOSITORY`)
67	
68	//axhos_step
69	. *Resolve Trigger Identity*:
70	.. {rbbc_store}
71	`«TRIGGER_NAME»` from `RBGC_RUBRIC_TRIGGER_PREFIX` + `«VESSEL_SIGIL»`
72	.. {rbbc_call}
73	link:https://cloud.google.com/build/docs/api/reference/rest/v1/projects.locations.triggers/list[cloudbuild.triggers.list] with:
74	... project:
75	`«DEPOT_PROJECT_ID»`
76	... location:
77	{rbrr_gcp_region}
78	... filter matching `«TRIGGER_NAME»`
79	.. {rbbc_require}
80	exactly one trigger matches
81	.. {rbbc_store}
82	`«TRIGGER_ID»` from matching trigger resource
83	.. {rbbc_fatal}
84	if not found: "Vessel trigger not found"
85	
86	//axhos_step
87	. *Dispatch Build via Trigger*:
88	.. {rbbc_store}
89	`«RUBRIC_COMMIT»` as HEAD of the build definition repository
90	.. {rbbc_submit}
91	link:https://cloud.google.com/build/docs/api/reference/rest/v1/projects.locations.triggers/run[cloudbuild.triggers.run] with:
92	... project:
93	`«DEPOT_PROJECT_ID»`
94	... location:
95	{rbrr_gcp_region}
96	... triggerId: `«TRIGGER_ID»`
97	... source.repoSource.commitSha: `«RUBRIC_COMMIT»`
98	.. Zero custom substitution overrides — all `_RBGY_*` values baked in
99	{rbtgr_build_json}
100	.. {rbbc_require}
101	HTTP 200 response
102	.. {rbbc_store}
103	`«BUILD_ID»` from response `.metadata.build.id`
104	.. {rbbc_show}
105	build dispatched with `«BUILD_ID»`
106	.. {rbbc_show}
107	Cloud Console URL for build monitoring
108	
109	//axhos_step
110	. *Cloud Build Execution*:
111	.. NOTE: Build executes via OCI Layout Bridge pattern to solve BuildKit credential isolation. See Trade Study RBSOB for design rationale.
112	.. {gcb_service}
113	executes the following phases within the
114	{rbtgi_depot_project}:
115	... *Build Phase*: Multi-platform container compilation produces OCI Image Layout artifact in shared workspace. No registry credentials required.
116	... *Push Phase*: OCI artifact copied to
117	{rbtgi_repository}
118	using
119	{oauth_token}
120	obtained from
121	{gcb_service}
122	metadata server.
123	... *Platform Split Phase*: Multi-platform OCI layout split to single-platform (linux/amd64) via skopeo (per-platform tag isolation).
124	... *Manifest Assembly Phase*: Consumer-facing multi-platform manifest list assembled from per-platform tags.
125	.. All phases execute as
126	{rbtr_mason}
127	within the depot infrastructure.
128	
129	//axhos_step
130	. *Wait for Build Completion*:
131	.. {rbbc_poll}
132	link:https://cloud.google.com/build/docs/api/reference/rest/v1/projects.locations.builds/get[cloudbuild.builds.get] with:
133	... project:
134	`«DEPOT_PROJECT_ID»`
135	... location:
136	{rbrr_gcp_region}
137	... buildId: `«BUILD_ID»`
138	.. Poll every 5 seconds for up to 20 minutes (240 attempts)
139	.. {rbbc_store}
140	`«BUILD_STATUS»` from response `.status`
141	.. Continue polling while `«BUILD_STATUS»` in [`PENDING`, `QUEUED`, `WORKING`]
142	.. {rbbc_fatal}
143	if timeout: "Build timeout after 20 minutes"
144	.. {rbbc_fatal}
145	if final status is not `SUCCESS`: "Build failed with status: «BUILD_STATUS»"
146	
147	//axhoot_typed_output
148	{rbtga_hallmark}
149	. *Display Results*:
150	.. {rbbc_show}
151	build completed successfully
152	.. {rbbc_show}
153	{rbtga_hallmark}
154	tag (dual-timestamp: inscribe + realized)
155	.. {rbbc_show}
156	image available in
157	{rbtgi_repository}
158	
159	//axhoc_completion
160	Successful execution builds the container image and stores it in the repository.
161	{rbtgr_provenance}
162	is auto-generated by Google as a signed SLSA v1.0 attestation on the built image.
163	Proceed to
164	{rbtgo_ark_about}
165	to assemble metadata, then
166	{rbtgo_ark_vouch}
167	to verify provenance.
168	
169	==== Substitution Variable Inventory
170	
171	All `_RBGY_*` substitution variables are baked into
172	{rbtgr_build_json}
173	at inscribe time.
174	Cloud Build resolves these from the committed JSON — no values are overridden at dispatch.
175	
176	*Vessel identity*::
177	[cols="1,2"]
178	|===
179	| `_RBGY_MONIKER` | Vessel sigil. Source: {rbrv_sigil}.
180	| `_RBGY_DOCKERFILE` | Dockerfile path relative to build context. Source: {rbrv_conjure_dockerfile}.
181	| `_RBGY_PLATFORMS` | Target platforms. Source: {rbrv_conjure_platforms}.
182	|===
183	
184	*Registry coordinates*::
185	[cols="1,2"]
186	|===
187	| `_RBGY_GAR_LOCATION` | Artifact Registry region. Source: {rbrr_gcp_region}.
188	| `_RBGY_GAR_PROJECT` | Depot project ID. Source: derived `RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID` from `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` + `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`.
189	| `_RBGY_GAR_REPOSITORY` | GAR repository name. Source: derived `RBDC_GAR_REPOSITORY` from `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` + `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`.
190	| `_RBGY_GAR_HOST_SUFFIX` | GAR Docker host suffix. Source: RBGC constant.
191	| `_RBGY_ARK_BASENAME_IMAGE` | Image artifact basename under the hallmark subtree. Source: RBGC constant.
192	| `_RBGY_CRANE_TAR_GZ` | Crane release tarball URL. Source: RBGC constant.
193	|===
194	
195	*Provenance*::
196	[cols="1,2"]
197	|===
198	| `_RBGY_GIT_COMMIT` | Main repo HEAD SHA. Placeholder in generated JSON; filled at inscribe time.
199	| `_RBGY_GIT_BRANCH` | Main repo branch at inscribe time.
200	| `_RBGY_GIT_REPO` | Main repo identifier (owner/repo).
201	| `_RBGY_RUBRIC_REPO` | Build definition repository URL. Placeholder in generated JSON; filled at inscribe time.
202	| `_RBGY_RUBRIC_COMMIT` | Build definition repository HEAD SHA after push. Placeholder in generated JSON; filled at inscribe time.
203	| `_RBGY_INSCRIBE_TIMESTAMP` | Inscribe timestamp (`YYMMDDHHMMSS`). Placeholder in generated JSON; filled at inscribe time. Used by rbgjb01 to construct {rbtga_hallmark}.
204	|===
205	
206	Builder image references (step `name:` fields) are resolved to digest-pinned URIs from regime pins at inscribe time.
207	These are inlined directly into JSON — not expressed as substitution variables.
208	
209	==== Script Inlining
210	
211	Step scripts (`rbgjb/*.sh`) are the source of truth for build logic.
212	At stitch time, each script is read and inlined into the corresponding JSON `args` array of
213	{rbtgr_build_json}.
214	
215	Developers edit `rbgjb/*.sh` in the main repo, then run the stitch function to regenerate
216	{rbtgr_build_json}
217	with the updated content.
218	
219	==== Tag Construction
220	
221	Each build produces a
222	{rbtga_hallmark}
223	with dual timestamps:
224	
225	`c«YYMMDDHHMMSS»-r«YYMMDDHHMMSS»`
226	
227	* `c«YYMMDDHHMMSS»` — conjure mode prefix + inscribe timestamp, baked as `_RBGY_INSCRIBE_TIMESTAMP`
228	* `r«YYMMDDHHMMSS»` — realized timestamp, derived at build time by `rbgjb01-derive-tag-base.sh`
229	
230	Example: `c260224153022-r260224160530`
231	
232	Step `rbgjb01` constructs: `HALLMARK="c${_RBGY_INSCRIBE_TIMESTAMP}-r$(date -u +%y%m%d%H%M%S)"`
233	
234	Complete artifact references compose the hallmarks root, the hallmark identifier, and the ark basename with {rbtga_hallmark} as tag:
235	
236	* {rbtga_ark_image}: `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/«HALLMARK»/«ARK_BASENAME_IMAGE»:«HALLMARK»`
237	
238	About and vouch artifacts are created by subsequent pipeline stages ({rbtgo_ark_about}, {rbtgo_ark_vouch}).
239	
240	Multiple builds of the same inscribed JSON sort together by inscribe prefix,
241	each distinguished by its realized timestamp.
242

1	==== Summary
2	
3	NOTE: SUPERSEDED (₣Av): Trigger dispatch eliminated entirely. Builds now use `builds.create` with pouch for context delivery. This trade study is retained as historical context for the CB v2 connection decision, which itself is now eliminated.
4	
5	The `triggers.run` REST API cannot dispatch builds against Developer Connect
6	repositories. The API's `RunBuildTriggerRequest.source` field only accepts
7	`RepoSource` — a 1st generation Cloud Source Repositories type. This is a
8	structural limitation in the protobuf schema, not a bug or misconfiguration.
9	
10	The solution is to replace Developer Connect connections with Cloud Build v2
11	connections and repositories, which are the supported path for trigger-based
12	dispatch via `triggers.run` + `RepoSource`.
13	
14	==== Root Cause
15	
16	The `projects.triggers.run` REST API accepts a `RunBuildTriggerRequest` whose
17	`source` field is typed as `RepoSource`. This type belongs to the 1st generation
18	Cloud Source Repositories (CSR) resource model. It cannot reference Developer
19	Connect `gitRepositoryLinks` or any 2nd generation repository type.
20	
21	When a trigger is configured with a Developer Connect repository and invoked via
22	`triggers.run`, the API ignores all `RepoSource` fields in the request body.
23	The result is an HTTP 400 error: `"Couldn't read commit "` (empty commit string)
24	— the API received no usable source reference.
25	
26	This is confirmed by the Go GAPIC protobuf surface (Dec 2025), which shows
27	`RunBuildTriggerRequest.source` typed as `RepoSource` with no alternative.
28	
29	==== Two Resource Hierarchies
30	
31	Google Cloud has two separate, non-interchangeable repository connection systems:
32	
33	[cols="1,1,1",options="header"]
34	|===
35	|Aspect
36	|Developer Connect
37	|Cloud Build v2
38	
39	|API
40	|`developerconnect.googleapis.com`
41	|`cloudbuild.googleapis.com/v2`
42	
43	|Resource
44	|`gitRepositoryLinks`
45	|`repositories`
46	
47	|Connection type
48	|Developer Connect connection
49	|CB v2 connection
50	
51	|Trigger compatibility
52	|Cannot be used with `triggers.run`
53	|Works with `triggers.run` + `RepoSource`
54	|===
55	
56	Developer Connect connections and Cloud Build v2 connections are separate APIs
57	with separate resource models. A trigger must be backed by a CB v2 repository
58	for `triggers.run` dispatch to function.
59	
60	==== Provenance Implications
61	
62	SLSA v1.0 provenance generation on Cloud Build is tied to the build invocation
63	method:
64	
65	* **Trigger-invoked builds** (`triggers.run` or webhook/push events) produce
66	  SLSA v1.0 provenance attestations automatically
67	* **Manual builds** (`builds.create`) produce only v0.1 provenance — insufficient
68	  for SLSA v1.0 compliance
69	
70	Since Tier 2 of the Cloud Build migration requires SLSA v1.0 provenance,
71	trigger-based dispatch is mandatory. The `builds.create` path cannot be used
72	as a fallback.
73	
74	==== Decision
75	
76	Replace Developer Connect with Cloud Build v2 connections and repositories.
77	
78	The `triggers.run` + `RepoSource` combination is the supported path for
79	programmatic trigger dispatch against CB v2 repository-backed triggers.
80	Developer Connect `gitRepositoryLinks` are not usable for this purpose.
81	
82	This changes the paddock's infrastructure design:
83	
84	* **Delete**: Developer Connect connection creation from `rbgp_depot_levy()`
85	* **Delete**: Developer Connect OAuth flow from `rbgm_depot_initialize()`
86	* **Add**: CB v2 connection and repository creation
87	* **Update**: Linked terms from `rbtgi_source_connection` (Developer Connect)
88	  to CB v2 equivalents
89	* **Retain**: Direct `builds.create` submission with pouch-based context delivery
90	
91	==== Experimental Evidence
92	
93	Three `triggers.run` request body formats were tested against a trigger
94	configured with a Developer Connect repository. All produced HTTP 400.
95	
96	[cols="1,2,1",options="header"]
97	|===
98	|Format
99	|Body
100	|Result
101	
102	|RepoSource with branch
103	|`{"source": {"repoSource": {"branchName": "main"}}}`
104	|HTTP 400: `"Couldn't read commit "`
105	
106	|RepoSource with commit
107	|`{"source": {"repoSource": {"commitSha": "<sha>"}}}`
108	|HTTP 400: `"Couldn't read commit "`
109	
110	|Empty source
111	|`{}`
112	|HTTP 400: `"Couldn't read commit "`
113	|===
114	
115	In all cases, the error message contains an empty commit string — the API
116	received the `RepoSource` fields but could not resolve them against the
117	Developer Connect repository backing the trigger.
118	
119	For comparison, `builds.create` with `developerConnectConfig` succeeded
120	(HTTP 200) but produced only v0.1 provenance — confirming that Developer
121	Connect itself works, but only via the non-trigger path that lacks SLSA v1.0.
122	
123	==== Web Research References
124	
125	[cols="1,3",options="header"]
126	|===
127	|Topic
128	|Source
129	
130	|RunBuildTriggerRequest.source is RepoSource (Go GAPIC proto)
131	|https://pkg.go.dev/cloud.google.com/go/cloudbuild/apiv1/v2/cloudbuildpb#RunBuildTriggerRequest[RunBuildTriggerRequest protobuf definition]
132	
133	|Source type supports connected_repository and git_source (Go GAPIC proto)
134	|https://pkg.go.dev/cloud.google.com/go/cloudbuild/apiv1/v2/cloudbuildpb#Source[Source protobuf definition — shows alternatives not available in RunBuildTriggerRequest]
135	
136	|Feature request for 2nd gen support in trigger/build requests
137	|https://github.com/googleapis/google-cloud-go/issues/9029[googleapis/google-cloud-go#9029]
138	
139	|Provenance tied to trigger-invoked builds (v1.0 vs v0.1)
140	|https://cloud.google.com/build/docs/securing-builds/generate-validate-build-provenance[Generate and validate build provenance]

file.content:
==== Summary

NOTE: SUPERSEDED (₣Av): Trigger dispatch eliminated entirely. Builds now use `builds.create` with pouch for context delivery. This trade study is retained as historical context for the CB v2 connection decision, which itself is now eliminated.

The `triggers.run` REST API cannot dispatch builds against Developer Connect
repositories. The API's `RunBuildTriggerRequest.source` field only accepts
`RepoSource` — a 1st generation Cloud Source Repositories type. This is a
structural limitation in the protobuf schema, not a bug or misconfiguration.

The solution is to replace Developer Connect connections with Cloud Build v2
connections and repositories, which are the supported path for trigger-based
dispatch via `triggers.run` + `RepoSource`.

==== Root Cause

The `projects.triggers.run` REST API accepts a `RunBuildTriggerRequest` whose
`source` field is typed as `RepoSource`. This type belongs to the 1st generation
Cloud Source Repositories (CSR) resource model. It cannot reference Developer
Connect `gitRepositoryLinks` or any 2nd generation repository type.

When a trigger is configured with a Developer Connect repository and invoked via
`triggers.run`, the API ignores all `RepoSource` fields in the request body.
The result is an HTTP 400 error: `"Couldn't read commit "` (empty commit string)
— the API received no usable source reference.

This is confirmed by the Go GAPIC protobuf surface (Dec 2025), which shows
`RunBuildTriggerRequest.source` typed as `RepoSource` with no alternative.

==== Two Resource Hierarchies

Google Cloud has two separate, non-interchangeable repository connection systems:

[cols="1,1,1",options="header"]
|===
|Aspect
|Developer Connect
|Cloud Build v2

|API
|`developerconnect.googleapis.com`
|`cloudbuild.googleapis.com/v2`

|Resource
|`gitRepositoryLinks`
|`repositories`

|Connection type
|Developer Connect connection
|CB v2 connection

|Trigger compatibility
|Cannot be used with `triggers.run`
|Works with `triggers.run` + `RepoSource`
|===

Developer Connect connections and Cloud Build v2 connections are separate APIs
with separate resource models. A trigger must be backed by a CB v2 repository
for `triggers.run` dispatch to function.

==== Provenance Implications

SLSA v1.0 provenance generation on Cloud Build is tied to the build invocation
method:

* **Trigger-invoked builds** (`triggers.run` or webhook/push events) produce
  SLSA v1.0 provenance attestations automatically
* **Manual builds** (`builds.create`) produce only v0.1 provenance — insufficient
  for SLSA v1.0 compliance

Since Tier 2 of the Cloud Build migration requires SLSA v1.0 provenance,
trigger-based dispatch is mandatory. The `builds.create` path cannot be used
as a fallback.

==== Decision

Replace Developer Connect with Cloud Build v2 connections and repositories.

The `triggers.run` + `RepoSource` combination is the supported path for
programmatic trigger dispatch against CB v2 repository-backed triggers.
Developer Connect `gitRepositoryLinks` are not usable for this purpose.

This changes the paddock's infrastructure design:

* **Delete**: Developer Connect connection creation from `rbgp_depot_levy()`
* **Delete**: Developer Connect OAuth flow from `rbgm_depot_initialize()`
* **Add**: CB v2 connection and repository creation
* **Update**: Linked terms from `rbtgi_source_connection` (Developer Connect)
  to CB v2 equivalents
* **Retain**: Direct `builds.create` submission with pouch-based context delivery

==== Experimental Evidence

Three `triggers.run` request body formats were tested against a trigger
configured with a Developer Connect repository. All produced HTTP 400.

[cols="1,2,1",options="header"]
|===
|Format
|Body
|Result

|RepoSource with branch
|`{"source": {"repoSource": {"branchName": "main"}}}`
|HTTP 400: `"Couldn't read commit "`

|RepoSource with commit
|`{"source": {"repoSource": {"commitSha": "<sha>"}}}`
|HTTP 400: `"Couldn't read commit "`

|Empty source
|`{}`
|HTTP 400: `"Couldn't read commit "`
|===

In all cases, the error message contains an empty commit string — the API
received the `RepoSource` fields but could not resolve them against the
Developer Connect repository backing the trigger.

For comparison, `builds.create` with `developerConnectConfig` succeeded
(HTTP 200) but produced only v0.1 provenance — confirming that Developer
Connect itself works, but only via the non-trigger path that lacks SLSA v1.0.

==== Web Research References

[cols="1,3",options="header"]
|===
|Topic
|Source

|RunBuildTriggerRequest.source is RepoSource (Go GAPIC proto)
|https://pkg.go.dev/cloud.google.com/go/cloudbuild/apiv1/v2/cloudbuildpb#RunBuildTriggerRequest[RunBuildTriggerRequest protobuf definition]

|Source type supports connected_repository and git_source (Go GAPIC proto)
|https://pkg.go.dev/cloud.google.com/go/cloudbuild/apiv1/v2/cloudbuildpb#Source[Source protobuf definition — shows alternatives not available in RunBuildTriggerRequest]

|Feature request for 2nd gen support in trigger/build requests
|https://github.com/googleapis/google-cloud-go/issues/9029[googleapis/google-cloud-go#9029]

|Provenance tied to trigger-invoked builds (v1.0 vs v0.1)
|https://cloud.google.com/build/docs/securing-builds/generate-validate-build-provenance[Generate and validate build provenance]

content.content:
1	//axhob_operation
2	{rbtgo_ark_conjure}
3	
4	NOTE: This operation conjures an
5	{rbtga_ark_image}
6	in
7	{rbtgi_repository}
8	by building a container image from a vessel specification. The
9	{rbtr_director}
10	performs the build via
11	{gcb_service}
12	using direct `builds.create` API submission.
13	
14	NOTE: Conjure produces both the image and about artifacts in a single Cloud Build job submitted via `builds.create`.
15	The about steps (`rbgja01`-`rbgja04`) are embedded via stitch alongside the build steps.
16	The vouch artifact is created by the subsequent {rbtgo_ark_vouch} pipeline stage (always a separate Cloud Build job).
17	
18	NOTE: The
19	{rbtga_hallmark}
20	is minted locally by the
21	{rbtr_director}
22	before submission.
23	The Director knows the complete artifact tag set before the build begins -- naming authority and verification authority are the same entity.
24	
25	NOTE: The conjure build job runs on the worker pool selected by the vessel's `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`: tether pool for vessels needing public internet (e.g., `apt-get` in Dockerfile), airgap pool for vessels with all dependencies enshrined in GAR.
26	The subsequent {rbtgo_ark_vouch} always runs on the airgap pool regardless of vessel egress mode.
27	
28	NOTE: *Universal reliquary precondition*. Every ordain-path vessel — conjure, bind, and graft — must have `RBRV_RELIQUARY` non-empty before this operation runs. The about/vouch pipeline (gcloud, docker, alpine, syft) consumes reliquary tool images uniformly across all modes; the registry preflight below enforces this.
29	
30	NOTE: The conjure Cloud Build job carries the {rbsk_in_pool_preflight} as its step 0, ahead of the extract-context step. Conjure populates `_RBGR_ENSHRINE_LOCATOR_n` substitutions from `RBRV_IMAGE_n_ANCHOR` slots, so the preflight HEAD-checks each anchored base in addition to the reliquary tools.
31	
32	The conjure operation:
33	
34	//axhopt_typed_parameter
35	{rbtga_ark}
36	. *Validate Input Parameters*:
37	.. {rbbc_require}
38	vessel provided as first argument
39	.. {rbbc_require}
40	vessel contains conjuring configuration (RBRV_CONJURE_DOCKERFILE, RBRV_CONJURE_BLDCONTEXT)
41	
42	//axhos_step
43	. *Registry Preflight*:
44	.. *Reliquary check*: HEAD request to `«RELIQUARY»/docker:latest` manifest in
45	{gar_service}.
46	{rbbc_require}
47	HTTP 200 — on 404, fail with directive to run inscribe first.
48	The reliquary is a co-versioned set of builder tool images inscribed from upstream into private GAR. Each vessel declares its reliquary; multiple datestamped reliquaries can coexist.
49	.. *Enshrine check*: For each indexed slot (n = 1, 2, 3) where both
50	{rbrv_image_origin}
51	and
52	{rbrv_image_anchor}
53	are set:
54	... Parse
55	{rbrv_image_anchor}
56	as a
57	{rbst_locator}
58	into package-path and tag
59	... HEAD request to `«LOCATOR-PATH»:«LOCATOR-TAG»` manifest in
60	{gar_service}
61	... {rbbc_require}
62	HTTP 200 — on 404, fail with directive to run {rbtgo_ark_enshrine} first.
63	Multiple vessels sharing the same anchor need only one enshrine.
64	.. Preflight prevents wasted
65	{gcb_service}
66	submission time on missing images, with copy-paste remediation commands
67	
68	//axhos_step
69	. *Resolve Base Images*:
70	.. For each indexed slot (n = 1, 2, 3) where
71	{rbrv_image_origin}
72	is set:
73	... If
74	{rbrv_image_anchor}
75	is present: parse the
76	{rbst_locator}
77	and resolve to full GAR image reference (`«GAR-REPO-BASE»/«LOCATOR-PATH»:«LOCATOR-TAG»`)
78	... If
79	{rbrv_image_anchor}
80	is absent: pass
81	{rbrv_image_origin}
82	through as the upstream tag (development path)
83	.. Resolved references become `RBF_IMAGE_n` build-args for the Dockerfile `ARG`/`FROM` pattern
84	
85	//axhos_step
86	. *Assign Hallmark*:
87	.. Mint
88	{rbtga_hallmark}
89	locally on the Director's workstation
90	.. {rbbc_store}
91	`«HALLMARK»` -- complete artifact tag set is now known before build submission
92	
93	//axhos_step
94	. *Push Pouch*:
95	.. Package build context (Dockerfile and context files) as a FROM SCRATCH OCI image
96	.. Push to GAR tagged `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/«HALLMARK»/pouch:«HALLMARK»`
97	.. {rbbc_require}
98	push succeeds -- the pouch delivers build context to
99	{gcb_service}
100	
101	//axhos_step
102	. *Execute Build*:
103	.. Stitch
104	{rbtgr_build_json}
105	with all step image references and substitution variables
106	.. {rbbc_submit}
107	`POST «GCB_PROJECT_BUILDS_URL»` with Build resource JSON via `builds.create`
108	.. All build parameters in stitched
109	{rbtgr_build_json}
110	-- zero custom substitution overrides
111	.. Waits for build completion
112	.. On success, the
113	{rbtga_ark_image}
114	artifact is created with the
115	{rbtga_hallmark}
116	.. {rbtgr_provenance}
117	is auto-generated by Google as a signed attestation on the built image
118	
119	//axhoot_typed_output
120	{rbtga_ark_image}
121	
122	//axhoot_typed_output axd_fact
123	{rbf_fact_hallmark}
124	The full
125	{rbtga_hallmark}
126	string.
127	
128	//axhoot_typed_output axd_fact
129	{rbf_fact_build_id}
130	The
131	{gcb_build}
132	job ID from the `builds.create` submission.
133	Used by
134	{rbtgo_ark_vouch}
135	for
136	{rbtgr_provenance}
137	verification.
138	
139	//axhoot_typed_output axd_fact
140	{rbf_fact_gar_root}
141	{gar_service}
142	registry prefix.
143	Composable with
144	{rbf_fact_ark_stem}
145	for full artifact references.
146	
147	//axhoot_typed_output axd_fact
148	{rbf_fact_ark_stem}
149	Hallmark subtree prefix joining
150	{gar_service}
151	root with the hallmark path segment as `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/«HALLMARK»`.
152	Composable with basename and `:«HALLMARK»` tag for full artifact references.
153	
154	//axhoot_typed_output axd_fact
155	{rbf_fact_ark_yield}
156	Per-platform
157	{rbtga_ark_image}
158	reference.
159	One fact file per platform discovered during the build.
160	
161	. *Display Results*:
162	.. {rbbc_show}
163	vessel image built with hallmark tag
164	
165	//axhoc_completion
166	Successful execution creates the
167	{rbtga_ark_image}
168	in
169	{rbtgi_repository}.
170	The {rbtr_director} retains `«BUILD_ID»` and `«HALLMARK»` from the conjure round-trip.
171	The `builds.create` job produces both the image and about artifacts under the hallmark subtree (about steps embedded via stitch).
172	{rbtgo_ark_ordain} chains only {rbtgo_ark_vouch} after the combined job completes, passing `«BUILD_ID»` for DSSE provenance verification.
173

lastPrompt: I'd like your help thinking through and defining a 'style guide' for a particular character of sequence: an orchestration sequence.  I've done several of these and I'm starting to have opinions about…

content.text:
Located four candidates and confirmed three are the orchestration sequence specs you're asking about. RBSCTD turned out to be a trade study (superseded), not a sequence spec — leaving three precedents.

**The three orchestration sequences:**

1. **BUSJGW-GarrisonWsl.adoc** (Windows garrison) — uses `{busc_*}` control verbs (BUS Console Control, parallel to `{rbbc_*}`)
2. **RBSTB-trigger_build.adoc** (Cloud Build trigger dispatch) — uses `{rbbc_*}`
3. **RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc** (Cloud Build container build) — uses `{rbbc_*}`

**Shared skeleton across all three:**
- `//axhob_operation` — operation header
- `//axhopt_typed_parameter` — typed inputs
- `//axhoq_precondition` — preconditions
- `//axhos_step` — numbered steps (substeps via `..`)
- `//axhoot_typed_output` (with optional `axd_fact` discriminator)
- `//axhog_guarantee` — on-success guarantees
- `//axhoc_completion` — exit criteria

**Shared control vocabulary** (defined RBS0:428-440, mirrored as `busc_*`):
`CALL`, `SUBMIT`, `POLL`, `AWAIT`, `REQUIRE`, `STORE`, `USE`, `FATAL`, `WARN`, `SHOW`, `VARIABLE`, `PROMPT`

**Runtime-value notation:** `«FOO»` guillemets for bound runtime values, backticks for literal syntax, `{linked_term}` for concept refs.

**Where they diverge in voice:**
- **RBSTB** is the most telegraphic — short bullets, dense control verbs, link to GCB REST surface (`link:https://.../triggers.run[...]`), trailing reference appendices (Substitution Inventory, Tag Construction).
- **RBSAC** is medium — `NOTE:` preludes do heavy cross-cutting lifting before the steps; steps lean prose-y for the build phases.
- **BUSJGW** is the most prose-heavy — long architectural narrative inside steps (e.g., the 5-phase obliterate is essentially a sub-spec embedded in step 4), with WSp-105 compliance language threaded through. The control verbs appear but are diluted by the surrounding rationale.

Three working hypotheses about what may be worth pinning down in your style guide, before I read more deeply:

1. **In-step prose budget** — RBSTB and RBSAC keep step bodies near-skeletal; BUSJGW pushes mechanism-rationale into substeps. Worth deciding whether rationale belongs in head NOTEs (RBSAC pattern) or inline (BUSJGW pattern).
2. **Control-verb density vs. natural-language fallthrough** — some substeps in BUSJGW have no control verb at all (e.g., "register the resulting SID under `HKLM\...`"). Either the vocabulary is incomplete, or we tolerate prose-only substeps. That's a real decision.
3. **Trailing reference material** — RBSTB has substantive appendices below `axhoc_completion`. BUSJGW does not. The convention for "where does ancillary material live" is undefined.

Which of these do you want to dig into first, or do you have a different cut you want to start from?

message.content:
1) I personally prefer splitting out rationale and supporting sources so that sequences are crystal clear and precise.
2) I'm content to diverge here: control verbs are critical when there are cross linkages to other procedures; here this is a 'one time setup' process which doesn't tie to others.
3) Well BUSJGW is accumulating a number of references now, is it not?

Lets add another consideration: I deeply want your help scribing, debugging, and precisely matching implementation and testing to a clear concise specification.  Thus, approaches that make it 'easiest for you to understand' for these goals is a premium.  

Thoughts?

content.text:
Agreement on (1) and (3); pushback on (2) worth surfacing; then four things you didn't ask about that I'd want in a style guide because they directly affect my ability to scribe/debug/test.

**On (2) — control verbs even for one-time setup.** When a substep reads "register the resulting SID under HKLM\..." with no control verb, I can't tell what role it plays: assertion to test, side-effect to observe, or precondition for the next step. Each `REQUIRE` becomes a test assertion; each `STORE` becomes an observable state variable; each `FATAL` becomes a negative test case. Dense control verbs are how I mechanically derive test plans from a spec. The honest counter is your point: if the test contract for a one-time setup is "did the sibling invigilate pass?", maybe verb dilution is earned because we're not building per-step tests anyway. But that earned-or-not decision should be explicit in the style guide, not implicit in author voice.

**What I'd want for scribe/debug/test fidelity:**

**A. Every FATAL names a scope.** BUSJGW's `{busc_fatal} on X — {bust_handbook_windows} scope (operator-managed)` is the strongest pattern in any of the three specs. It turns failures into routing decisions, not just exits. Promote this from BUSJGW convention to style rule.

**B. Trailing Runtime Values table.** Every `«FOO»` introduced, its origin step, its consumer steps. RBSTB has this for `_RBGY_*` substitutions but not for runtime values. A canonical table gives me a direct binding map from spec → implementation variable → test fixture. Cheap to maintain, very high signal.

**C. Step granularity stance.** A "step" should be one atomic unit — succeeds entirely or the operation fails at that boundary. BUSJGW step 4 is really five steps (the five phases each have their own FATAL); they're substeps only because the spec calls them phases. If the test contract is per-phase, they should be steps. If the contract is "obliterate as a whole", the current shape is right. The rule isn't which shape — it's that step boundaries == failure/test boundaries, and authors declare which they're optimizing.

**D. Step body cap as a smell.** Practical: when a step body grows past ~15 visible lines, it's a signal that something wants to extract — phases to sibling spec, rationale to head NOTE, references to trailing section, or one step into several. Not a hard limit, but a useful pressure.

**Concretely on BUSJGW**, if I were applying the above: head NOTEs trimmed to invariants only (the three current ones are mechanism narrative — they belong in a sibling rationale doc); inline Microsoft Learn / Win32-OpenSSH / helgeklein references hoisted into a trailing References section per RBSCTD's pattern; step 4's five phases promoted to top-level steps (4-8) since each has independent FATAL semantics; remaining substeps where they currently lack verbs get them.

Want me to sketch the style guide as a document, or work BUSJGW into the proposed shape as a worked example first?

message.content:
2) Aha!  Yes I do agree with this- I confused 'control verb' with 'general quoin': yes I do want to use what you are calling control verbs well here.  Read AXLA: we will need to consider some of its framing soon.

A) Fatal is one of my favorites for terse specification, yes I want to use this.  I don't fully understand your 'scoping' comment: Explain?  Or trust that eventually we can codify and add scoping rules later.

B) Would you show me an example?

C) Yes definitely, finer grained steps.  Also note WSG that helps us deal with the oddities of powershell by serious restriction.

D) yes, I agree.

Thoughts?

content.text: Reading AXLA and WSG so I can give grounded answers.

content.content:
515	== Axial Control Terms
516	
517	[[axc_variable]]
518	{axc_variable}::
519	A
520	{axl_motif}
521	representing a temporary value holder denoted by distinctive delimiters that exists for the duration of an operational sequence.
522	Implementation varies by context: shell variables in bash, dictionary entries in Python, sessionStorage in browser JavaScript.
523	
524	[[axc_fatal]]
525	{axc_fatal}::
526	A
527	{axl_motif}
528	representing immediate termination of the operational sequence with error status, preventing all subsequent steps from executing.
529	Requires operator intervention or error recovery before retry.
530	
531	[[axc_warn]]
532	{axc_warn}::
533	A
534	{axl_motif}
535	representing display of a non-blocking warning message while allowing the operational sequence to continue execution.
536	Alerts operators to concerning conditions without halting progress.
537	
538	[[axc_store]]
539	{axc_store}::
540	A
541	{axl_motif}
542	representing capture of a value from an operation result or computation into a
543	{axc_variable}
544	for later reference.
545	Context determines storage mechanism while preserving semantic intent.
546	
547	[[axc_use]]
548	{axc_use}::
549	A
550	{axl_motif}
551	representing reference to a previously stored
552	{axc_variable}
553	value in the current operation.
554	Retrieves stored values from context-appropriate storage location.
555	
556	[[axc_require]]
557	{axc_require}::
558	A
559	{axl_motif}
560	representing validation check that confirms an expected condition, triggering
561	{axc_fatal}
562	or alternate execution path if not met.
563	Enforces preconditions and invariants throughout execution.
564	
565	[[axc_poll]]
566	{axc_poll}::
567	A
568	{axl_motif}
569	representing repeated execution of a status check at defined intervals until completion condition or timeout.
570	Implements retry logic with configurable intervals and limits.
571	
572	[[axc_call]]
573	{axc_call}::
574	A
575	{axl_motif}
576	representing direct invocation of an external service, API endpoint, or command.
577	Captures both success and error responses for processing.
578	
579	[[axc_submit]]
580	{axc_submit}::
581	A
582	{axl_motif}
583	representing initiation of an asynchronous operation that returns immediately with a handle for later completion tracking.
584	Distinct from
585	{axc_call}
586	which expects synchronous response.
587	
588	[[axc_await]]
589	{axc_await}::
590	A
591	{axl_motif}
592	representing blocking wait on a submitted asynchronous operation until completion.
593	Polls operation status until done, then returns final result or error.
594	
595	[[axc_show]]
596	{axc_show}::
597	A
598	{axl_motif}
599	representing presentation of operational facts or values to the operator.
600	Implementation varies from console output to UI display based on context.
601	
602	[[axc_step]]
603	{axc_step}::
604	A
605	{axl_motif}
606	representing a normal progress message indicating successful completion of a processing stage.
607	Provides operational visibility without implying warnings or errors.
608	
609	== Axial Environment Terms
610	
611	{axe_environment_s}
612	specify execution contexts for control operations.
613	{axl_voicing_s}
614	of
615	{axc_call},
616	{axc_warn},
617	{axc_fatal}
618	and similar control motifs describe step-level behavior within operations.
619	
620	[[axe_rest_api]]
621	{axe_rest_api}::
622	A
623	{axl_motif}
624	representing invocation of an HTTP REST API endpoint.
625	Request/response via JSON or other structured format.
626	Authentication via bearer tokens, API keys, or similar.
627	Calls return HTTP status codes and response bodies.
628	
629	[[axe_console]]
630	{axe_console}::
631	A
632	{axl_motif}
633	representing direct terminal invocation.
634	Standard output and standard error are visible to the user.
635	Output can be routed directly to stdout/stderr.
636	
637	[[axe_mcp_transport]]
638	{axe_mcp_transport}::
639	A
640	{axl_motif}
641	representing invocation through the MCP (Model Context Protocol) transport layer.
642	Standard error is swallowed by the transport — all output must be captured in a return buffer to reach the caller.
643	
644	[[axe_daemon_runtime]]
645	{axe_daemon_runtime}::
646	A
647	{axl_motif}
648	representing a long-running system process.
649	Errors are logged, not displayed.
650	Fatal means graceful shutdown with cleanup.
651	No human in immediate feedback loop.
652	
653	== Axial Operation Terms
654	
655	[[axo_procedure]]
656	{axo_procedure}::
657	A
658	{axl_motif}
659	representing an executable operation with defined inputs, behavior, outputs, and completion semantics.
660	Standalone operations not affiliated with an entity.
661	Voiced at the
662	{axl_definition_site}
663	via
664	{axvo_procedure}.
665	
666	[[axo_role]]
667	{axo_role}::
668	A
669	{axl_motif}
670	representing an operational identity pattern that defines capabilities and permissions.
671	Determines available operations and access boundaries.
672	
673	[[axo_identity]]
674	{axo_identity}::
675	A
676	{axl_motif}
677	representing the abstract concept of an operational identity.
678	An entity capable of authentication and holding authorities within a system.
679	{axo_identity}
680	is the pattern;
681	{axo_actor_s}
682	are named instantiations with specific bindings.
683	
684	[[axo_actor]]
